coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence enhanced route to detect copper(II) by click chemistry-catalyzed connection of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yuan
Ma, Zhanfang
coredata.dc:description: Pursuing for a simple, sensitive and inexpensive method of detecting copper ions is very essential for both environment and human health. Herein, a new strategy for fluorescence detection of Cu2+ was designed which is based on the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ by sodium ascorbate to get cycloaddition reaction between 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 and propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots (CDs). The proposed approach not only possesses a good selectivity, but also has a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0.1–100nM with the detection limit of 0.08nM (5.12×10−9 mgmL−1), which is far below the limit value of 1.3mgmL−1 defined by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinkable water. In the actual sample, the standard deviation ranges from 97.6% to 101.8%, indicating the high precision of present approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper ion
Click chemistry
Fluorescence detection
Au@SiO2 core shell
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots
Characterization of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots
Analytical performance of the Cu2+ detection sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Base Construction Projects of Beijing Municipal Education Commission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financed by Grants from the Beijing Natural Science Foundation ( 2132008 ), the Research Base Construction Projects of Beijing Municipal Education Commission . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ based on click chemistry.
TEM image of Au@SiO2 (A) and UV–vis spectra (B) of AuNPs (a) and Au@SiO2 (b).
HRTEM image of CDs. Inset: the lattice spacing of CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of AuNPs-CDs and Au@SiO2-CDs.

(A) Emission spectra of the fluorescence sensor after the addition of Cu2+ with different concentrations. From the bottom to top: 0, 0.1, 10, 20, 40, 50, 80 and 100nM. (B) The corresponding calibration plot of fluorescence intensity vs. the concentration of Cu2+.
The selectivity of the method for Cu2+ (100nM) against other metal ions with concentration of 100μM.
Results of the Cu2+ recovery experiments performed in river water samples.
Comparison of different methods for Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence enhanced route to detect copper(II) by click chemistry-catalyzed connection of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy for fluorescence detection of Cu2+ was designed which is based on the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ by sodium ascorbate to get cycloaddition reaction between 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 and propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots.

Pursuing for a simple, sensitive and inexpensive method of detecting copper ions is very essential for both environment and human health. Herein, a new strategy for fluorescence detection of Cu2+ was designed which is based on the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ by sodium ascorbate to get cycloaddition reaction between 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 and propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots (CDs). The proposed approach not only possesses a good selectivity, but also has a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0.1–100nM with the detection limit of 0.08nM (5.12×10−9 mgmL−1), which is far below the limit value of 1.3mgmL−1 defined by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinkable water. In the actual sample, the standard deviation ranges from 97.6% to 101.8%, indicating the high precision of present approach.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions pollution has long been considered as one of the world-wide concerns in the last century due to the industrialization and improper waste disposal, particularly in regions where the anthropogenic activities are poorly controlled [1–3]. The heavy metal ions, which are non-biodegradable, can cause human health problems when present in water. As a generally used metal, copper is an essential trace element in maintaining the normal operation of the human organs and keeping health. A small amount of intake of copper ions is beneficial to human body. However, excessive intake can show high toxicity and be harmful to gastrointestinal system, liver and kidneys, and even damage central nervous system [4–6]. Over the past years, researchers have developed a number of methods to detect metal ions, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [7], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [8], visual detection [9,10], chemiluminescence detection [11] and electrochemical techniques [12,13]. Nevertheless, these methods have some disadvantages such as expensive instrument, complicated sample preparations and tedious operation procedures, resulting in the need for other sensitive, simple detection method of copper ions.

In numerous detection methods, fluorescence detection has witnessed an explosion in recent years [14–16]. Fluorescence materials of detection such as carbon dots (CDs) was utilized widely to the detection of heavy metal ion due to low cost, high sensitivity, good chemical stability, easy preparation, and tunable excitation and emission spectra [17–21]. Cuprous ions-catalyze azide and alkyne cycloaddition reaction (CuAAC) with facile and gentle experimental condition belongs to the typical click chemistry reaction, exhibiting good efficiency and selectivity. It has been reported that the fluorescence signals can be significantly enhanced when metal nanoparticle and fluorophores are interacted, which represents the typical metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) technology [22]. In addition, it is very common to apply CuAAC reaction into Cu2+ detection via colorimetric assays or DNA assays [23–26]. Although other groups have detected Cu2+ via fluorescence detection method [27,28], there are no reports combining metal-enhanced fluorescence technology and click chemistry reaction to detect copper ions.

Herein, a new fluorescence enhanced sensing method was developed by using CDs as signal and Au nanoparticles for signal enhancement to sensitively detect copper ions. Cu(I) ions deriving from the reduction of Cu(II) ions by sodium ascorbate can trigger CuAAC reaction. Present approach of Cu2+ detection relies on the fact that Cu+ catalyzes cycloaddition of azides groups on Au@SiO2 and alkynes groups on CDs. The present approach showed a high performance towards Cu2+ detection.

Hydrogen Tetrachloroaurate hydrate (HAuCl4·xH2O), sodium ascorbate, and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTMS) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. 4-Azidobenzoic acid and propiolic acid were achieved from Aladdin. 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), branched Polyethylenimine (BPEI) and Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH) and sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), AgNO3, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, KNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, FeCl2·4H2O and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu science and technology development limited company. Nylon Springe Filter (0.45μm) was purchased from Membrane Solutions Company. Ultrapure water (resistivity >18MΩ) was used throughout the experiments. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

In all the procedures, the water used was purified by an Olst ultrapure K8 apparatus (Olst, Ltd., resistivity >18MΩ). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a JEOL-100CX electron microscope under 80kV accelerating voltage. Absorption spectra were measured with a 2550 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All the Fluorescence spectra were measured from F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (AXimA-CFR, Hitachi, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were acquired on a JEOL-2100 electron microscope.

In a typical synthesis, 15nm AuNPs were synthesized according to previous work [29–32]. 4mL of 1% sodium citrate solution was added to 100mL of 0.01% boiling HAuCl4 solution. After additional 30min, the heating is stopped and the 15nm AuNPs were obtained.

The solution (1mL) which contained 12.8mg polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (average molar masses of 55000kgmol−1) was not added until it cooled to room temperature, then the mixed solution was stirred for another 36h [33]. After several times centrifuging and washing with deionized-distilled water, the resulting PVP-modified AuNPs was re-dispersed in 30mL deionized-distilled water.

Thereafter, 20mL isopropyl alcohol, 6mL PVP-modified AuNPs and 0.35mL ammonia were added into conical flask under vigorous magnetic stirring. After stirring for 30min, the 2mL isopropyl alcohol containing 5 microliters tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was added dropwise into the flask. It required another 4h to complete the whole preparation process. In order to ensure the 4-azidobenzoic acid be modified on the surface of silicon dioxide through the 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide, n-hydroxysuccinimide (EDC/NHS), amino propyl trimethoxy silane (APTMS) was applied to change the hydroxyl into amino group on the silicon surface. 1 microliters APTMS was added into the Au@SiO2 solution which was centrifuged and re-dispersed in 6mL isopropyl alcohol. After stirring vigorously for 12h, above solution was centrifuged, washed and dispersed into 6mL water. 0.2mmol 4-azidobenzoic acid solution (pH 8) containing 0.2mmol EDC was added to the already prepared Au@SiO2 solution reacting for 48h at least before use [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The branched polyethylenimine (BPEI)-CDs were synthesized by a facile green microwave-assisted pyrolysis method according to previous work with a slight modification [35]. In a typical experiment, 0.3g BPEI and 0.1g citric acid monohydrate (CA) were mixed in 4mL deionized water. The mixture solution reacted in microwave reaction instrument (300W) at 195°C for 15min, and then cooled down to room temperature. The resulting CDs were dialyzed against water using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut off (MWCO)=1000Da) for three days to remove excess CA and BPEI. 0.2mmol propiolic acid solution containing 0.2mmol DCC and 0.2mmol NHS was stirred and added to the already prepared BPEI-CDs reacting for 48h at least before use [36].

Our procedures of sensitive fluorescent detection of Cu2+ were listed as follows: different concentrations of copper ions and 5 times molar concentrations of sodium ascorbate were dissolved in a centrifuge tube which contained 0.5mL azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 solution and 1.5mL propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots. After stirred about 3h, the conjugates were centrifuged and washed several times by deionized water to remove unattached CDs, then re-dispersed in 1mL deionized water before fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared AuNPs were sphere particles with a diameter of about 15nm (Fig. 1 A), which was accord with the UV–vis absorption maximum around 519nm from Fig. 1B(a). When the thickness of silica shell is ∼10nm, the absorbance peak showed obvious red-shifts to 527nm compared to uncoated AuNPs (Fig. 1B(b)). The reason is the intensity of the plasmon absorption band increased [37]. As shown in Fig. S1, amino groups were modified on the surface of Au@SiO2 which changed their zeta potential from −16.9mV to +18.9mV in ultra-pure water. A peak appeared at 2130cm−1 (Fig. S2), indicating that the azide groups were introduced.

An improved microwave-assisted method according to the previous literature [35] was used to prepare CDs. CA served as the carbon source and BPEI as the reducing agent to synthesize CDs possessing blue fluorescence. Fig. 2 showed the HRTEM image of CDs and the corresponding particle size distribution. The as-prepared CDs were monodispersed nanocrystals with a diameter of about 6nm.

The fluorescence emission intensity of AuNPs-CDs and Au@SiO2-CDs was shown in Fig. 3 . The excitation wavelength is 300nm and emission wavelength 380nm. When Cu2+ of 100nM was added into the solutions of AuNPs-CDs and Au@SiO2-CDs, respectively, different results were produced. It did have some enhancement of fluorescence intensity when CDs was connected to Au@SiO2, yet the fluorescence was quenched when the CDs was directly connected to AuNPs. The fluorescence intensity was approximately 10-fold higher for AuNPs@SiO2-CDs than for AuNPs-CDs. Metal-enhanced fluorescence is an established theory. Fluorescence enhancement was caused by the interactions between fluorophores and noble metal nanoparticles. The silicon dioxide with 10nm thickness was used in this assay because the silicon dioxide can produce the strongest fluorescence enhancement [22].

By means of amination reaction, Au@SiO2 was modified with azide and alkynes groups on CDs (Scheme 1 ). Upon the addition of Cu+ deriving from the reduction of Cu2+, the click chemistry reaction was triggered between terminal azide groups of Au@SiO2 and alkyne groups of CDs. As a result, CDs was linked with Au@SiO2 and fluorescence signal of CDs was amplified by Au core through controlling the distance between of AuNPs and CDs. Therefore, a new fluorescence enhanced sensing method to detect Cu2+ was developed, and the concentration of Cu2+ was directly proportional to the fluorescence intensity of CDs connected with AuNPs effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the Cu2+ detection sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased at 446nm when the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 0.1 to 100nM. In the absence of Cu2+, low fluorescence intensity was measured because propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots and 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 nanoparticles could not be connected. As the concentration of the Cu+ increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased. These results showed that the cycloaddition reaction occurred where propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots were connected with 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 nanoparticles. Fig. 4B outlined the relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ and fluorescence intensity. The detection limit of present method is 0.08nM. A linear relationship was determined to be 0.1–100nM (R2 =0.9989).

In order to examine the selectivity of the proposed assay against Cu2+, 100μM other heavy metal ions including Fe2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, K+, Fe3+, Cd2+ and Ag+ were detected separately. As depicted in Fig. 5 , no remarkable fluorescence intensity was measured compared the addition of 100nM Cu2+. It obtained the superior selectivity against other interfering metal ions in the fluorescence detection for Cu2+. Stability also had vital significance in evaluating the property of the sensor. The sensor was stored at room temperature when not in use. The fluorescence intensity had no significant change after storing for 15 days, representing that this sensor has a good stability (Fig. S3).

To further test the applicability for practical samples, the standard addition method was used in present assay. The river water samples were filtered through 0.45μm filter membrane to remove any large suspended particles, then added copper ions with concentration of 25 and 50nM. As illustrated in Table 1 , it was of good coincident between the concentrations of Cu2+ detected through present approach and the concentration of Cu2+ spiked. This confirmed that present assay can be of vital accuracy in practical applications for Cu2+ detection. Many successful method of detecting Cu2+ has been fabricated in recent years as depicted in Table 2 . The present approach obtains higher sensitivity and lower detection limit.

In summary, we have designed a new strategy for Cu2+ detection in aqueous solution based on Cu+-catalyzed click chemistry. A new nanohybrid system is composed of 4-azidobenzoic acid-tagged Au@SiO2 and propiolic acid-tagged carbon dots. This system detecting Cu2+ has been implemented by reducing Cu2+ into Cu+ to catalyze the cycloaddition of the terminal azide groups and alkyne groups. At the present assay, the silicon shell with a certain thickness plays an important role to amplify the fluorescence signal of CDs instead of any enzyme amplification. Aside from the applications of Cu2+ detection, continuous work is being made to achieve small biomolecules and other pollutants detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by Grants from the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2132008), the Research Base Construction Projects of Beijing Municipal Education Commission.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.112.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuan Zhou received B.S. degree from Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology in 2014. Now she is a graduate student at Capital Normal University. Her research focused on the heavy metal ion detection.

Zhanfang Ma received a B.S. degree from Northeast Normal University and a Ph.D. in colloid and interface science in Key Laboratory of Colloid, Interface and Thermodynamics, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is currently a full professor of physical chemistry in Department of Chemistry of Capital Normal University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor, nanofabrication, and electrochemical biosensors.
